clean_story:
Water from thin air. Trees drinking clouds.

The term fog feeding describes how forests pull moisture directly from mist—like sipping through a straw instead of waiting for rain. Coastal redwoods and Chilean Nothofagus trees do this best. Their needles or leaves act as tiny hooks, snagging fog droplets that merge into heavier beads. When enough collect, they drip to the roots below.

One cypress in California’s Santa Cruz Mountains can harvest over two hundred liters of water per day this way. The fog condenses on its branches like dew on grass at dawn, then funnels downward. Without this trick, many dry-season forests would thirst. Even lichens and mosses rely on it, soaking up mist like sponges left in a damp sink.

Three related subjects are cloud forest ecosystems, how desert plants trap dew, and the physics of condensation.
